t.headline: Burstable Performance for Variable Workloads
t.reasoning: T-series instances use a CPU credit model that provides a baseline level of CPU performance with the ability to burst above baseline. This makes them ideal for workloads that don't use the full CPU consistently but occasionally need high CPU performance.

m.headline: Balanced Compute, Memory & Networking
m.reasoning: M-series instances provide a balance of compute, memory, and networking resources. They are the most versatile instance family and are suitable for a wide range of workloads. If you're unsure which instance type to choose, M-series is a safe starting point.

a.headline: Cost-Effective Arm-Based General Purpose
a.reasoning: A-series instances are powered by AWS Graviton processors (Arm-based). They deliver up to 20% lower cost compared to x86-based instances for scale-out and Arm-compatible workloads.

mac.headline: macOS on AWS for Apple Development
mac.reasoning: Mac instances are powered by Apple Mac mini hardware. They are the only way to run macOS in the cloud for building, testing, and signing Apple applications. Required for iOS/macOS CI/CD pipelines.

c.headline: Highest Per-Core CPU Performance
c.reasoning: C-series instances are optimized for compute-bound applications that benefit from high-performance processors. They have the highest ratio of vCPU to memory, making them ideal for CPU-intensive tasks where raw compute power matters most.

r.headline: High Memory for Data-Intensive Workloads
r.reasoning: R-series instances are designed for memory-intensive applications. They provide more memory per vCPU than general purpose instances, making them ideal for workloads that process large data sets in memory. The 'R' stands for RAM.
not_for: CPU-bound workloads with low memory needs

x.headline: Extreme Memory for Largest In-Memory Databases
x.reasoning: X-series instances offer the highest memory capacity in EC2. They are purpose-built for large-scale, enterprise-class in-memory applications like SAP HANA. If your workload requires terabytes of RAM, X-series is your only option.

z.headline: Highest Clock Speed + High Memory
z.reasoning: Z-series instances combine high single-thread performance with high memory. They use custom Intel Xeon processors with sustained all-core turbo boost up to 4.5 GHz. Ideal for workloads that need fast single-threaded performance AND large memory.

i.headline: High Random I/O & NVMe SSD Storage
i.reasoning: I-series instances are optimized for workloads requiring high random I/O performance and high sequential read throughput. They come with NVMe-based local instance storage, delivering very low latency.
not_for: Workloads that need persistent storage (data is ephemeral!)
CPU-bound workloads with no storage needs

d.headline: Massive HDD Storage at Lowest Cost
d.reasoning: D-series instances provide dense, cost-effective HDD-based local storage. They are optimized for sequential read/write workloads where the cost per TB is critical. Perfect for massive data lakes.

h.headline: High Sequential Throughput with HDD
h.reasoning: H-series instances are built for workloads requiring high-throughput access to large sequential data. They complement D-series with higher memory and networking for throughput-critical scenarios.

p.headline: NVIDIA GPU for ML Training & HPC
p.reasoning: P-series instances are powered by NVIDIA GPUs (V100, A100, H100) designed for machine learning training, deep learning, and HPC. They provide the highest GPU compute performance in EC2.

g.headline: GPU for Graphics, Rendering & Inference
g.reasoning: G-series instances provide GPU acceleration for graphics-intensive applications and ML inference. They balance GPU compute with graphics rendering capabilities using NVIDIA T4/A10G GPUs.

inf.headline: AWS Inferentia for Cost-Effective ML Inference
inf.reasoning: Inf-series instances use AWS's custom Inferentia chips, designed specifically for ML inference. They deliver up to 2.3x higher throughput and up to 70% lower cost per inference compared to GPU-based instances.

trn.headline: AWS Trainium for Cost-Effective ML Training
trn.reasoning: Trn-series instances use AWS's custom Trainium chips, purpose-built for deep learning training. They deliver up to 50% cost savings over comparable GPU instances for training large models.

hpc.headline: Purpose-Built for High Performance Computing
hpc.reasoning: HPC instances are specifically designed for tightly-coupled HPC workloads. They offer high-bandwidth, low-latency EFA networking and optimized compute for simulation and modeling.

db.t.headline: Burstable DB Instances for Variable Workloads
db.t.reasoning: db.t instances use a CPU credit system, ideal for databases with variable workloads that don't need sustained high CPU. They're the most cost-effective option for development, testing, and small production databases.

db.m.headline: Balanced Performance for Most DB Workloads
db.m.reasoning: db.m instances offer a balance of compute, memory, and networking suitable for the majority of production database workloads. They provide consistent performance without the burst model, making them predictable and reliable.

db.r.headline: High Memory for Data-Intensive Databases
db.r.reasoning: db.r instances are designed for memory-intensive database workloads. They provide more GiB of memory per vCPU than db.m instances, allowing more data to be cached in memory, reducing disk I/O and improving query performance dramatically.

db.x.headline: Extreme Memory for Largest Databases
db.x.reasoning: db.x instances offer the highest memory capacity for RDS. They are purpose-built for demanding database workloads that require terabytes of memory, such as large Oracle or SQL Server databases.

mysql, postgres, mariadb, oracle-ee, sqlserver-ee, aurora-mysql, aurora-postgresql

mysql.headline: World's Most Popular Open-Source Database
mysql.reasoning: MySQL is the most widely adopted open-source relational database. Choose MySQL when you need a proven, cost-effective RDBMS with a massive community, extensive tooling, and broad compatibility.

postgres.headline: Advanced Open-Source Object-Relational DB
postgres.reasoning: PostgreSQL is the most advanced open-source relational database. Choose PostgreSQL when you need advanced data types (JSON, arrays, hstore), complex queries, strong ACID compliance, and extensibility.

aurora-mysql.headline: MySQL-Compatible, 5x Faster Performance
aurora-mysql.reasoning: Aurora MySQL is AWS's cloud-native MySQL-compatible database. It delivers up to 5x the throughput of standard MySQL with automatic scaling, high availability (6-way replication), and seamless migration from standard MySQL.

aurora-postgresql.headline: PostgreSQL-Compatible, 3x Faster Performance
aurora-postgresql.reasoning: Aurora PostgreSQL delivers up to 3x the throughput of standard PostgreSQL. It combines PostgreSQL compatibility with Aurora's cloud-native architecture for automatic scaling and high availability.

mariadb.headline: Enhanced MySQL Fork with Extra Features
mariadb.reasoning: MariaDB is a community-driven fork of MySQL with additional storage engines, optimizations, and features. Choose MariaDB when you want MySQL compatibility with extra open-source features.

oracle-ee.headline: Enterprise-Grade Commercial RDBMS
oracle-ee.reasoning: Oracle is the industry-standard enterprise database. Choose Oracle on RDS when your application requires Oracle-specific features like PL/SQL, RAC compatibility, or you have existing Oracle licenses.

sqlserver-ee.headline: Microsoft's Enterprise Database on AWS
sqlserver-ee.reasoning: SQL Server on RDS is ideal for Microsoft-stack applications. Choose SQL Server when your application depends on T-SQL, SSRS, SSIS, or .NET integration.
